---
title: "Bayesian Inference and Bayesian Statistics"
subtitle: "A rigorous long-form course in probability, inference, computation, modeling, diagnostics, and modern probabilistic workflow"
toc: false
page-layout: full
---

::: {.hero}
# Bayesian Inference and Bayesian Statistics

This website is designed as a serious university-level course in Bayesian statistics for advanced undergraduates, MSc students, PhD students, and quantitatively mature self-learners. The emphasis is unapologetically mathematical, but the pedagogy is computationally grounded: every major theme is connected to executable implementations in `R`, `Stan`, and `JAX`, with careful attention to diagnostics, tuning, model criticism, and reproducibility.

The organizing principle is that Bayesian statistics is not merely a formula for updating beliefs. It is a coherent workflow that begins with probability models, proceeds through prior design and posterior computation, and ends with decision-relevant summaries, predictive performance, and model criticism [@bernardo1994; @gelman2013; @betancourt2017].
:::

## Course Purpose

This course has four simultaneous goals:

1. Develop the formal probability and statistical foundations needed for mathematically precise Bayesian reasoning.
2. Derive core Bayesian inferential results carefully, including conjugate posteriors, marginal likelihoods, posterior predictive distributions, and decision-theoretic estimators.
3. Build modern computational competence, from Monte Carlo integration and MCMC to HMC, variational inference, state-space methods, and gradient-based inference.
4. Train a disciplined Bayesian workflow in `R`, `Stan`, and `JAX`, including prior predictive checks, posterior predictive checks, diagnostics, sensitivity analysis, and reproducible reporting.

## What The Course Covers

::: {.grid-cards}
::: {.course-card}
### Part I
Probability foundations, likelihood theory, Bayes' theorem, exchangeability, sequential updating, and the logic of probabilistic learning.
:::

::: {.course-card}
### Part II
Conjugate analysis, loss functions, decision theory, prior design, shrinkage, prior predictive analysis, and sensitivity.
:::

::: {.course-card}
### Part III
Numerical approximation, Monte Carlo, MCMC, HMC, NUTS, variational inference, sequential Monte Carlo, and online Bayesian computation.
:::

::: {.course-card}
### Part IV
Linear models, GLMs, hierarchical models, mixture models, time series, Gaussian processes, Bayesian nonparametrics, spatial models, and causal perspectives.
:::

::: {.course-card}
### Part V
Optimization, MAP estimation, tuning, calibration, posterior diagnostics, model criticism, and predictive evaluation.
:::

::: {.course-card}
### Part VI-VII
Full workflows in `R`, `Stan`, and `JAX`, then advanced topics: model averaging, robustness, experimental design, communication, and capstone projects.
:::
:::

## Audience And Prerequisites

The intended audience should already be comfortable with:

- calculus through multivariable differentiation and integration;
- linear algebra at the level of eigenvalues, quadratic forms, and matrix factorizations;
- undergraduate probability and statistics;
- basic programming in `R` or `Python`.

The appendices provide a compact review of notation, measure-theoretic probability, and software setup:

- [Notation and assumptions](appendix/notation.qmd)
- [Measure-probability review](appendix/measure-probability-review.qmd)
- [Software setup](appendix/software-setup.qmd)

## Course Design Principles

Each major topic is organized around a repeating pedagogical template:

1. Intuition and motivating problem.
2. Formal definition and notation.
3. Core theorem or structural result.
4. Step-by-step derivation.
5. Worked example.
6. Computational implementation.
7. Diagnostics, pathologies, and common mistakes.
8. Exercises.
9. Reading notes and references.

This repeated structure is intentional. Bayesian statistics becomes much easier to master when definitions, derivations, computation, and criticism are presented as a single coherent object rather than as disconnected topics.

## Representative Deep-Dive Chapters

The site includes several fully written exemplar chapters that demonstrate the intended level of mathematical and computational detail for the rest of the course:

- [Bayes' theorem, sequential updating, and posterior prediction](chapters/foundations/bayes-theorem-and-updating.qmd)
- [Numerical integration and approximation](chapters/computation/numerical-integration-and-approximation.qmd)
- [Markov chain Monte Carlo: derivation, tuning, and diagnostics](chapters/computation/markov-chain-monte-carlo.qmd)
- [Advanced MCMC: HMC, NUTS, geometry, and diagnostics](chapters/computation/advanced-mcmc-hmc.qmd)
- [Variational inference: ELBO and coordinate ascent](chapters/computation/variational-inference.qmd)
- [Sequential and online Bayes: filtering and particle methods](chapters/computation/sequential-and-online-bayes.qmd)
- [Stan workflow for hierarchical models and HMC diagnostics](chapters/software/stan-hierarchical-models.qmd)
- [JAX for gradient-based Bayesian computation](chapters/software/jax-gradient-based-bayes.qmd)

## Software Stack

The course uses three complementary tools.

### `R`

`R` is used for simulation, analytic conjugate calculations, custom samplers, posterior summaries, graphical diagnostics, and high-level workflow with `posterior`, `bayesplot`, `loo`, and `cmdstanr` [@r2025; @posterior2024; @bayesplot2024; @loo2024; @cmdstanr2024].

### `Stan`

`Stan` provides an industrial-strength implementation of HMC and NUTS, making it the natural vehicle for serious posterior computation in hierarchical and nonlinear models [@carpenter2017; @hoffman2014].

### `JAX`

`JAX` is introduced as Python-based infrastructure for automatic differentiation, vectorization, JIT compilation, optimization, and custom gradient-based Bayesian computation [@jax2018]. It is especially useful when one wants transparent control over objectives, gradients, Hessians, and algorithmic building blocks.

## Executable Labs

The website is static, but the repository is organized so that examples can be executed with minimal setup:

- `labs/r/beta_binomial.R`
- `labs/r/mh_sampler.R`
- `labs/stan/eight_schools.stan`
- `labs/r/fit_eight_schools.R`
- `labs/jax/jax_logistic_map_hmc.py`

The recommended hosting architecture is documented in [Deployment and online execution](deployment.qmd). The short version is:

- host the Quarto site on GitHub Pages;
- use Posit Cloud for `R` and `Stan` labs;
- use Binder or Codespaces for Python/JAX notebooks and scripts;
- keep all environments versioned in the repository.

## Reading Strategy

The course is intentionally layered.

- Readers with strong mathematical background can move quickly through Part I and focus on computation and modeling.
- Applied statisticians can begin with conjugate analysis, linear models, and workflow chapters, then circle back to measure-theoretic details.
- Researchers implementing custom algorithms should pair Part III with the JAX chapter and the diagnostics material in Part V.

## Quick Navigation

- [Course blueprint](course-blueprint.qmd)
- [Detailed syllabus and pacing](syllabus.qmd)
- [Chapter-by-chapter expansion map](chapter-outline.qmd)
- [Part I: Foundations](parts/part-i-foundations.qmd)
- [Part III: Computational Bayesian methods](parts/part-iii-computation.qmd)
- [Part VI: Software workflows](parts/part-vi-software-workflows.qmd)
- [Projects and capstones](projects.qmd)
- [References and citation strategy](references.qmd)

## Guiding Notation

Throughout the course:

- observed data are denoted by $y$ or $y_{1:n}$;
- parameters are denoted by $\theta$;
- priors by $\pi(\theta)$;
- likelihoods by $p(y \mid \theta)$;
- posteriors by $p(\theta \mid y)$;
- posterior predictive distributions by $p(\tilde{y} \mid y)$.

When conditioning sets become large, we privilege clarity over brevity and write full model factorizations explicitly. That is deliberate. In advanced Bayesian work, notation is not cosmetic; it is a safeguard against conceptual mistakes.
